content:
# Genesis Chapter 25 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 14r–14v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_025_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_025_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 21–22 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0025

---

*Abraham and Keturah — The Last Sons (vv. 1–4)*

**25:1** And Abraham [וְאַבְרָהָם] added [יָסַף] and took [וַיִּקַּח] a wife [אִשָּׁה] and her name [וּשְׁמָהּ] was Keturah [קְטוּרָה].

**25:2** And she bore [וַתֵּלֶד] to him [לוֹ] Zimran [אֶת-זִמְרָן] and Jokshan [וְאֶת-יָקְשָׁן] and Medan [וְאֶת-מֶדָן] and Midian [וְאֶת-מִדְיָן] and Ishbak [וְאֶת-יִשְׁבָּק] and Shuah [וְאֶת-שׁוּחַ].

**25:3** And Jokshan [וְיָקְשָׁן] fathered [יָלַד] Sheba [אֶת-שְׁבָא] and Dedan [וְאֶת-דְּדָן] — and the sons of Dedan [וּבְנֵי דְדָן] were Asshurim [הָיוּ אַשּׁוּרִם] and Letushim [וּלְטוּשִׁם] and Leummim [וּלְאֻמִּים].

**25:4** And the sons of Midian [וּבְנֵי מִדְיָן] — Ephah [עֵיפָה] and Epher [וָעֵפֶר] and Hanoch [וַחֲנֹךְ] and Abida [וַאֲבִידָע] and Eldaah [וְאֶלְדָּעָה] — all these [כָּל-אֵלֶּה] are the sons [בְּנֵי] of Keturah [קְטוּרָה].

---

*Abraham's Final Dispositions — Isaac and the Sons of the Concubines (vv. 5–6)*

**25:5** And Abraham gave [וַיִּתֵּן אַבְרָהָם] all [אֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ] that he had [לוֹ] to Isaac [לְיִצְחָק].

**25:6** And to the sons of the concubines [וְלִבְנֵי הַפִּילַגְשִׁים] who were Abraham's [אֲשֶׁר לְאַבְרָהָם] Abraham gave [נָתַן אַבְרָהָם] gifts [מַתָּנֹת] and sent them away [וַיְשַׁלְּחֵם] from upon Isaac [מֵעַל יִצְחָק] his son [בְּנוֹ] while he was still living [בְּעוֹדֶנּוּ חָי] — eastward [קֵדְמָה] — to the land of the east [אֶל-אֶרֶץ קֶדֶם].

---

*The Death of Abraham (vv. 7–11)*

**25:7** And these [וְאֵלֶּה] are the days [יְמֵי] of the years [שְׁנֵי] of the life of Abraham [חַיֵּי-אַבְרָהָם] that he lived [אֲשֶׁר-חָי] — one hundred [מְאַת] and seventy [שִׁבְעִים] and five [וְחָמֵשׁ] years [שָׁנָה].

**25:8** And Abraham breathed his last [וַיִּגְוַע אַבְרָהָם] and he died [וַיָּמָת] in a good old age [בְּשֵׂיבָה טוֹבָה] — old [זָקֵן] and satisfied [וְשָׂבֵעַ] — and he was gathered [וַיֵּאָסֶף] to his people [אֶל-עַמָּיו] [*ne'esaf el-'ammav*].

**25:9** And Isaac and Ishmael [וַיִּקְבְּרוּ אֹתוֹ יִצְחָק וְיִשְׁמָעֵאל] his sons buried him [בָּנָיו] in the cave of Machpelah [בִּמְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה] — in the field [בִּשְׂדֵה] of Ephron [עֶפְרֹן] the son of Zohar [בֶּן-צֹחַר] the Hittite [הַחִתִּי] — which is before [אֲשֶׁר עַל-פְּנֵי] Mamre [מַמְרֵא].

**25:10** The field [הַשָּׂדֶה] that Abraham purchased [אֲשֶׁר-קָנָה אַבְרָהָם] from the sons of Heth [מֵאֵת בְּנֵי-חֵת] — there [שָׁמָּה] Abraham was buried [קֻבַּר אַבְרָהָם] and Sarah [וְשָׂרָה] his wife [אִשְׁתּוֹ].

**25:11** And it happened [וַיְהִי] after [אַחֲרֵי] the death of Abraham [מוֹת אַבְרָהָם] that God blessed [בֵּרַךְ אֱלֹהִים] Isaac [אֶת-יִצְחָק] his son [בְּנוֹ] — and Isaac dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב יִצְחָק] by Beer-lahai-roi [עִם-בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי].

---

*The Toledot of Ishmael — His Sons and His Death (vv. 12–18)*

**25:12** And these [וְאֵלֶּה] are the generations [תֹּלְדֹת] of Ishmael [יִשְׁמָעֵאל] the son of Abraham [בֶּן-אַבְרָהָם] — whom Hagar [אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה הָגָר] the Egyptian [הַמִּצְרִית] — the female slave of Sarah [שִׁפְחַת שָׂרָה] — bore to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם].

**25:13** And these [וְאֵלֶּה] are the names [שְׁמוֹת] of the sons of Ishmael [בְּנֵי יִשְׁמָעֵאל] — by their names [בִּשְׁמֹתָם] — according to their generations [לְתֹלְדֹתָם] — the firstborn of Ishmael [בְּכֹר יִשְׁמָעֵאל] — Nebaioth [נְבָיֹת] — and Kedar [וְקֵדָר] and Adbeel [וְאַדְבְּאֵל] and Mibsam [וּמִבְשָׂם].

**25:14** And Mishma [וּמִשְׁמָע] and Dumah [וְדוּמָה] and Massa [וּמַשָּׂא].

**25:15** Hadad [חֲדַד] and Tema [וְתֵימָא] — Jetur [יְטוּר] — Naphish [נָפִישׁ] and Kedemah [וָקֵדְמָה].

**25:16** These [אֵלֶּה] are the sons of Ishmael [הֵם בְּנֵי יִשְׁמָעֵאל] and these [וְאֵלֶּה] are their names [שְׁמֹתָם] by their villages [בְּחַצְרֵיהֶם] and by their encampments [וּבְטִירֹתָם] — twelve [שְׁנֵים-עָשָׂר] princes [נְשִׂיאִם] according to their peoples [לְאֻמֹּתָם].

**25:17** And these [וְאֵלֶּה] are the years [שְׁנֵי] of the life [חַיֵּי] of Ishmael [יִשְׁמָעֵאל] — one hundred [מְאַת] and thirty [שְׁלֹשִׁים] and seven [וְשֶׁבַע] years [שָׁנָה] — and he breathed his last [וַיִּגְוַע] and he died [וַיָּמָת] and was gathered [וַיֵּאָסֶף] to his people [אֶל-עַמָּיו].

**25:18** And they settled [וַיִּשְׁכְּנוּ] from Havilah [מֵחֲוִילָה] to Shur [עַד-שׁוּר] — which is before [אֲשֶׁר עַל-פְּנֵי] Egypt [מִצְרַיִם] — toward Assyria [בֹּאֲכָה אַשּׁוּרָה] — over against all his brothers [עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-אֶחָיו] he fell [נָפָל] [*nafal*].

---

*The Toledot of Isaac — Rebekah's Barrenness and the Oracle (vv. 19–23)*

**25:19** And these [וְאֵלֶּה] are the generations [תֹּלְדֹת] of Isaac [יִצְחָק] the son of Abraham [בֶּן-אַבְרָהָם] — Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] fathered [הוֹלִיד] Isaac [אֶת-יִצְחָק].

**25:20** And Isaac [וַיְהִי יִצְחָק] was forty [בֶּן-אַרְבָּעִים] years old [שָׁנָה] when he took [בְּקַחְתּוֹ] Rebekah [אֶת-רִבְקָה] the daughter of Bethuel [בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל] the Aramean [הָאֲרַמִּי] from Paddan-aram [מִפַּדַּן אֲרָם] — the sister of Laban [אֲחוֹת לָבָן] the Aramean [הָאֲרַמִּי] — for himself as wife [לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה].

**25:21** And Isaac entreated the LORD [וַיֶּעְתַּר יִצְחָק לַיהוָה] on behalf of his wife [לְנֹכַח אִשְׁתּוֹ] because [כִּי] she was barren [עֲקָרָה הִוא] — and the LORD was entreated by him [וַיֵּעָתֶר לוֹ יְהוָה] [*vaye'ater lo*] — and Rebekah [וַתַּהַר רִבְקָה] his wife conceived [אִשְׁתּוֹ].

**25:22** And the children struggled together [וַיִּתְרֹצֲצוּ הַבָּנִים] within her [בְּקִרְבָּהּ] [*vayitrotsatsu*] — and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: If it be so [אִם-כֵּן] — why [לָמָּה] am I [זֶה אָנֹכִי]? And she went [וַתֵּלֶךְ] to inquire [לִדְרֹשׁ] of the LORD [אֶת-יְהוָה].

**25:23** And the LORD said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה] to her [לָהּ]:

Two nations [שְׁנֵי גֹיִים] are in your womb [בְּבִטְנֵךְ] —
and two peoples [וּשְׁנֵי לְאֻמִּים] from your bowels [מִמֵּעַיִךְ] shall be divided [יִפָּרֵדוּ] —
and [one] people [וּלְאֹם] shall be stronger than [the other] [מִלְאֹם יֶאֱמָץ] people —
and the greater [וְרַב] shall serve [יַעֲבֹד] the lesser [צָעִיר] [*rav ya'avod tsa'ir*].

---

*The Birth of Esau and Jacob (vv. 24–26)*

**25:24** And her days to give birth [וַיִּמְלְאוּ יָמֶיהָ לָלֶדֶת] were fulfilled — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] there were twins [תוֹמִם] in her womb [בְּבִטְנָהּ].

**25:25** And the first one [וַיֵּצֵא הָרִאשׁוֹן] came out [יָצָא] — all of him [כֻּלּוֹ] like a hairy [כְּאַדֶּרֶת שֵׂעָר] garment [שֵׂעָר] — and they called [וַיִּקְרְאוּ] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Esau [עֵשָׂו] ['admoni — *red/ruddy* — see note].

**25:26** And after that [וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן] came out [יָצָא] his brother [אָחִיו] — and his hand [וְיָדוֹ] was holding [אֹחֶזֶת] the heel [בַּעֲקֵב] of Esau [עֵשָׂו] — and his name [וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ] was called Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] [*ya'aqov — the heel-grasper*] — and Isaac [וְיִצְחָק] was sixty [בֶּן-שִׁשִּׁים] years old [שָׁנָה] when she bore them [בְּלֶדֶת אֹתָם].

---

*The Boys Grow — Esau the Hunter, Jacob the Tent-Dweller (vv. 27–28)*

**25:27** And the lads [וַיִּגְדְּלוּ הַנְּעָרִים] grew — and Esau [וַיְהִי עֵשָׂו] became [וַיְהִי] a man knowing hunting [אִישׁ יֹדֵעַ צַיִד] — a man of the field [אִישׁ שָׂדֶה] — and Jacob [וְיַעֲקֹב] was a complete [תָּם] man [אִישׁ] — dwelling in tents [יֹשֵׁב אֹהָלִים] [*tam — see note*].

**25:28** And Isaac [וַיֶּאֱהַב יִצְחָק] loved [אָהַב] Esau [אֶת-עֵשָׂו] because [כִּי] game [צַיִד] was in his mouth [בְּפִיו] — and Rebekah [וְרִבְקָה] loved [אֹהֶבֶת] Jacob [אֶת-יַעֲקֹב].

---

*Esau Sells His Birthright (vv. 29–34)*

**25:29** And Jacob [וַיָּזֶד יַעֲקֹב] boiled [בִּישֶּׁל] a boiling [נָזִיד] — and Esau [וַיָּבֹא עֵשָׂו] came from the field [מִן-הַשָּׂדֶה] and he [was] faint [הוּא עָיֵף].

**25:30** And Esau said [וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב]: Let me eat now [הַלְעִיטֵנִי נָא] from the red — the red [הָאָדֹם הָאָדֹם] — the this [הַזֶּה] [*ha'adom ha'adom*] — for I am faint [כִּי עָיֵף אָנֹכִי] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] his name was called [קָרָא-שְׁמוֹ] Edom [אֱדוֹם].

**25:31** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב]: Sell me [מִכְרָה כַיּוֹם] as of today [כַיּוֹם] your birthright [בְּכֹרָתְךָ] to me [לִי].

**25:32** And Esau said [וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו]: Behold [הִנֵּה] I am going to die [אָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ לָמוּת] — and what [וְלָמָּה-זֶּה] to me [לִי] is this birthright [בְּכֹרָה]?

**25:33** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב]: Swear to me [הִשָּׁבְעָה לִּי] as of today [כַּיּוֹם] — and he swore [וַיִּשָּׁבַע] to him [לוֹ] — and he sold [וַיִּמְכֹּר] his birthright [אֶת-בְּכֹרָתוֹ] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב].

**25:34** And Jacob [וְיַעֲקֹב] gave [נָתַן] to Esau [לְעֵשָׂו] bread [לֶחֶם] and a boiling of lentils [וּנְזִיד עֲדָשִׁים] — and he ate [וַיֹּאכַל] and he drank [וַיֵּשְׁתְּ] and he arose [וַיָּקָם] and he went [וַיֵּלַךְ] — and Esau [וַיִּבֶז עֵשָׂו] despised [בָּזָה] his birthright [אֶת-הַבְּכֹרָה].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 14r–14v:**
Genesis 25 begins on folio 14r — the final third of the right-hand column of the leaf whose verso (folio 13v) carried the bulk of Genesis 24. The Masoretic three-column layout is clear throughout both pages. The beginning of Genesis 25 falls immediately after the conclusion of chapter 24 without a new-page break, but a *parashah petuchah* (open section, פ) marker is visible in the text preceding vv. 1–6, correctly dividing the new narrative unit from the conclusion of the Rebekah story. The Ishmael genealogy section (vv. 12–18) carries its own *toledot* marker with a *setumah* (closed section, ס) before v. 12, appropriate to the transition into the genealogical register. The Isaac *toledot* opens at v. 19 with another open-section marker. Page 2 (folio 14v, inverted in the photograph — the image must be read rotated) carries the text from approximately v. 19 through v. 34 and then continues into Genesis 26. The Masoretic cantillation (*te'amim*) and vowel pointing (*nikud*) are clear and well-preserved on both pages. The Vaticanus pages 21–22 carry the LXX text of Genesis 25 in continuous uncial script across the three-column page layout; page 21 (online viewer 0025) opens mid-Genesis 24 with the heading *CAP. XXXVIII* visible at the top center, and carries Genesis 25 to approximately verse 20; page 22 (online viewer 0026) completes the chapter and opens into Genesis 26.

**25:1 — "Added and took" (*wayyōseph wayyiqqaḥ*, וַיֹּסֶף וַיִּקַּח):**
The verb *yāsap* (יָסַף, "to add, to again, to do again") functions here as an auxiliary: Abraham *again* took — he added to his previous marriages by taking another wife. This is the standard Hebrew idiom for a repeated action. The LXX Vaticanus reads *prosthemenos de Abraam* (προσθέμενος δὲ Αβρααμ) — "and Abraham adding [i.e., going on to]" — exactly equivalent to the MT *wayyōsep*. The marriage to Keturah is chronologically ambiguous: was it during Sarah's lifetime, after her death, or is this a retrospective summary? The narrative placement after Sarah's death and burial (ch. 23) and after the securing of Isaac's marriage (ch. 24) strongly implies a post-Sarah union. 1 Chronicles 1:32 calls Keturah Abraham's *concubine* (*pîlegeš*), not wife (*'iššāh*), possibly harmonizing the account with v. 6 of this chapter where Abraham's concubine-sons are sent away. The MT here uses *'iššāh* ("wife") without qualification. EMET follows the MT precisely.

**25:6 — "The sons of the concubines" (*bĕnê happîlagšîm*, בְּנֵי הַפִּילַגְשִׁים):**
The plural *pîlagšîm* ("concubines") is unexpected — Abraham is described as having only Hagar (explicitly named as *pîlegeš*, "concubine," at Gen 16:3) and Keturah (called *'iššāh*, "wife," at v. 1, but *pîlegeš* at 1 Chr 1:32). The plural form may be a compressed reference to both women (Hagar and Keturah), categorizing Keturah retroactively as a concubine. EMET renders the plural as "the sons of the concubines" following the MT exactly, without harmonization. The instruction to send these sons *east* (*qēdmāh*, "eastward") to "the land of the east" (*'ereṣ qedem*) — a term for the vast territory east and southeast of Canaan, including Arabia and Mesopotamia — is significant: Abraham actively clears the field so that Isaac alone inherits the covenantal land and promise. The gifts (*mattānōt*) compensate the other sons without giving them a covenantal share.

**25:7–8 — "Breathed his last" (*wayyigwa'*, וַיִּגְוַע) and "gathered to his people" (*ne'esaf el-'ammav*, נֶאֱסַף אֶל-עַמָּיו):**
The verb *gāwa'* (גָּוַע) is the specific verb for dying by the last breath — the body's final expiration. It is the solemn, physical word for death, distinct from the general *mût* ("die") which follows it: *wayyigwa' wayyāmot* — "he breathed his last and he died" — the two verbs together creating a formal death notice. The phrase *ne'esaf el-'ammav* — "he was gathered to his people" — is one of the Hebrew Bible's most evocative idioms for death. The Niphal of *'āsap* (אָסַף, "to gather, to collect") with the sense of being *gathered in* — as one gathers the last sheaves of a harvest — implies reunion with the ancestors who have gone before. Abraham is *added to* the assembly of his dead kin; he joins the company of those who preceded him. The idiom appears at the deaths of Abraham (here), Ishmael (v. 17), Isaac (35:29), Jacob (49:33), Aaron (Num 20:24), and Moses (Num 27:13). It implies a continuing existence in some form beyond death, though the Hebrew scriptures are characteristically restrained about the nature of that existence. The LXX Vaticanus renders *ne'esaf el-'ammav* as *prosetethē pros to genos autou* (προσετέθη πρὸς τὸ γένος αὐτοῦ) — "he was added to his kindred" — a precise equivalent.

**25:8 — "In a good old age, old and satisfied" (*bĕśêbāh ṭôbāh zāqēn wĕśāḇêa'*):**
The death notice for Abraham contains three descriptors: *śêbāh ṭôbāh* ("a good old age" — literally "good grayness"), *zāqēn* ("old/elder"), and *śāḇêa'* ("satisfied/full"). The accumulation is deliberate: this is the ideal death — long life, full experience, contentment. The same phrase *bĕśêbāh ṭôbāh* was promised to Abraham at the covenant of the pieces (Gen 15:15: *wĕ'attāh tābō' el-'abōtêkā bĕšālôm tiqqābēr bĕśêbāh ṭôbāh* — "and you shall go to your fathers in peace, you shall be buried in a good old age"). The narrative notes the precise fulfillment of that promise: what was prophesied at the covenant has now come to pass, word for word.

**25:9 — Isaac and Ishmael Together at the Burial:**
The narrator records, without comment, that *both* Isaac and Ishmael buried their father together. This is the only moment in the narrative where the two half-brothers appear side by side as joint actors. Ishmael, who was sent away as a child (Gen 21), has been absent from the story for chapters — yet he returns for his father's burial. The tomb itself is the cave of Machpelah, purchased from Ephron the Hittite in Genesis 23, where Sarah is already buried. The legal and topographical specificity (Ephron son of Zohar the Hittite, before Mamre) echoes the formal language of the purchase deed at Gen 23:17–20. The LXX Vaticanus follows the MT exactly, confirming both sons' presence.

**25:11 — "God" (not "the LORD") blesses Isaac:**
Note the shift in the divine name at v. 11: *bērak 'ĕlōhîm* ("God blessed") rather than the tetragrammaton *YHWH*. After the death notice formula closes the Abraham narrative, the new subsection opening Isaac's story introduces the general term *'ĕlōhîm* rather than the covenantal name. This may be a structural marker: the narrator uses the more universal divine name to introduce the transition to the next generation, before the covenant name resumes in the Isaac stories. EMET preserves "God" here and does not render it as "the LORD."

**25:12 — The First Toledot Formula of the Chapter:**
*Toledot* (תֹּלְדֹת) — "generations, genealogical record, offspring account" — is the major structural marker of Genesis. The formula *wĕ'elleh toledot X* ("and these are the generations of X") appears ten times in Genesis (2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10; 11:27; 25:12; 25:19; 36:1; 37:2), dividing the book into its major narrative units. Uniquely, Genesis 25 contains two *toledot* formulas: Ishmael's (v. 12) and Isaac's (v. 19). That Ishmael's *toledot* precedes Isaac's is significant structurally — the text dispenses with the non-covenantal line first (as it does with Esau in Genesis 36 before Jacob's *toledot* in 37:2), clearing the narrative field before focusing on the line of promise.

**25:18 — *Nafal* (נָפַל) — "He fell" / "He settled":**
Verse 18b reads *'al-pĕnê kol-'eḥāyw nāpal* — "over against all his brothers he fell." The verb *nāpal* (נָפַל, "to fall") is the same word used in the oracle over Ishmael in Gen 16:12: *wĕ'al-pĕnê kol-'eḥāyw yiškōn* — "and over against all his brothers he shall dwell." The parallel is almost exact but with one important difference: Gen 16:12 uses *yiškōn* ("he will dwell"), while here the text uses *nāpal* ("he fell"). Some interpreters read *nāpal* here in the sense of "to settle, to encamp" (a well attested usage: cf. Num 2:31; Josh 17:5), making the two passages near-synonymous: "he settled/encamped in the face of all his brothers." Others read it as the literal fall-verb, meaning Ishmael "fell" — died — in the presence of all his kin, consistent with the death notice in v. 17. The LXX Vaticanus reads *katenanti pantōn tōn adelphōn autou katōkēsen* (κατέναντι πάντων τῶν ἀδελφῶν αὐτοῦ κατῴκησεν) — "he settled/dwelt over against all his brothers" — choosing the "settling/dwelling" sense and thus aligning this passage with 16:12. EMET renders *nāpal* literally as "fell" and notes the ambiguity. The LXX's harmonizing choice to use "dwelt" is noted as a significant variant.

**25:21 — *Ye'ater* / *Vaye'ater lo* (וַיֵּעָתֶר לוֹ) — "The LORD was entreated by him":**
The verb *'ātar* (עָתַר, "to pray, to entreat, to supplicate") appears here in both the Qal (*wayyetar*, "he entreated") and Niphal (*wayyē'āter lô*, "he was entreated / allowed himself to be entreated") forms — an elegant grammatical chiasm: Isaac *entreated* the LORD, and the LORD *was entreated* by him. The Niphal of *'ātar* — "to allow oneself to be entreated, to relent, to respond to supplication" — is relatively rare in the Hebrew Bible (cf. 2 Sam 21:14; Ezra 8:23; 2 Chr 33:13). It carries the nuance of divine responsiveness: the LORD is not indifferent to Isaac's prayer but actively responds to it, relenting from the condition of barrenness. The word conveys not mere mechanical fulfillment but relational engagement — God hears and is moved. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eisēkousen autou ho theos* (εἰσήκουσεν αὐτοῦ ὁ θεός) — "God listened/heard him" — a valid but less precise equivalent; the specific *Niphal-of-'ātar* nuance of being actively persuaded or responsive is not fully preserved in the Greek.

**25:22 — *Vayitrotsatsu* (וַיִּתְרֹצֲצוּ) — "And they struggled together":**
The verb *rāṣaṣ* (רָצַץ, "to crush, to shatter, to violently press") in the Hitpoel stem here — *wayyitroṣaṣû* — describes the children's movement within the womb as violent, crushing, shattering motion. This is not gentle movement; the verb in its base form means to crush or oppress (cf. Judg 9:53, "she crushed his skull"; Isa 42:3, "a bruised reed"). The Hitpoel ("they crushed one another") adds a reciprocal, mutual-violence quality: the twins *crushed each other* within her. The word anticipates the conflictual relationship of the two nations they represent. EMET renders it "struggled together" — acknowledging the reciprocal force of the Hitpoel — but notes that the underlying Hebrew is far more physically violent than "struggled" conveys. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eskirtōn de ta paidia en autē* (ἐσκίρτων δὲ τὰ παιδία ἐν αὐτῇ) — "the children leaped/frolicked within her" — *skirtaō* is the verb used for the joyful leaping of the baby John in Elizabeth's womb (Luke 1:41). The LXX's choice *softens* the violent connotation dramatically; where the MT conveys crushing conflict, the LXX reads joyful movement. This is a theologically significant variant: EMET notes the MT's harsher reading as primary.

**25:22 — "If it be so, why am I?" (*'im-kēn lāmmāh zeh 'ānōkî*, אִם-כֵּן לָמָּה זֶה אָנֹכִי):**
Rebekah's exclamation is one of the most existentially compressed statements in the patriarchal narratives. The phrase is literally: "If so, why — this — I?" The full sense is something like: "If things are this way [with this crushing pain/conflict], why do I even exist / what is the purpose of my being?" The pronoun *'ānōkî* ("I") hangs bare at the end of the clause without a predicate — an anguished, elliptical cry of bewilderment and pain. Some translators supply the implicit predicate: "why is this happening to me?" or "why do I live?" The nakedness of the Hebrew — the bare *'ānōkî* with no following verb — is deliberate and EMET preserves it in translation with no supplement beyond what the Hebrew gives.

**25:23 — The Oracle — *Rav ya'avod tsa'ir* (וְרַב יַעֲבֹד צָעִיר) — Syntactic Ambiguity:**
The final line of the divine oracle is the most syntactically contested verse in the chapter. Hebrew word order is: *wĕrab ya'ăbōd ṣā'îr* — literally "and [the] great shall serve [the] small." *Rav* (רַב, "great, many, the greater/older") and *ṣā'îr* (צָעִיר, "small, younger, lesser") are both unmarked nouns — neither is grammatically marked as subject or object; in Hebrew, either can be the subject of *ya'ăbōd* ("shall serve"). The syntax genuinely permits two readings:

- Reading A: "The greater/older shall serve the younger/lesser" — meaning Esau will serve Jacob. This is the reading universally adopted by the NT (Rom 9:12), the Targums, Rashi, and most interpreters.
- Reading B: "The greater [nation] — the lesser shall serve him" — meaning Jacob's descendant-nation will serve Esau's. Grammatically possible but contextually nearly impossible given the subsequent narrative.

The first reading (A) is supported by: (1) the narrative fulfillment, where Jacob receives the blessing over Esau; (2) the historical pattern where Edom served Israel (under David, 2 Sam 8:14); (3) Paul's explicit citation in Rom 9:12 (*ho meizon douleusei tō elassoni*, "the greater shall serve the lesser"). The ambiguity is real but the context makes the intended sense clear. EMET preserves the ambiguity by translating the word order as literally as possible — "the greater shall serve the lesser" — and notes the syntactic issue. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ho meizōn douleusai tō elassoni* (ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι) — "the greater shall serve the lesser" — resolving in favor of Reading A with unambiguous Greek syntax (accusative case marking the subject).

**25:25 — *'Admoni* (אַדְמוֹנִי) and the Name "Esau":**
Verse 25 reads: *wayyēṣē' hārī'šôn 'admōnî kullô kĕ'aderet śē'ār wayyiqrĕ'û šĕmô 'ēśāw* — "And the first came out red/ruddy — all of him like a garment of hair — and they called his name Esau." The word *'admōnî* (אַדְמוֹנִי) means "red/ruddy" — from the root *'ādam* (אָדָם, "red"), the same root giving us *'ādām* (the human, "the red-earth one"), *'adāmāh* ("ground/soil"), and the place name *'Ĕdôm* (Edom). This child is red-colored and covered in hair like an animal skin. The name *'ēśāw* (עֵשָׂו) may relate to the root *'āśāh* ("to do, to make") or the Arabic cognate for "hairy." Importantly, Esau is not named *from his redness* at birth — he is named Esau. He is named *Edom* ("Red") only later at v. 30, from the red stew incident. The red color at birth and the red stew later create a verbal-thematic link: Esau is constituted as "red" (and associated with the impulsive appetite that will cost him everything) from the moment of his emergence. David also is described as *'admōnî* ("red/ruddy") at 1 Sam 16:12, suggesting attractiveness rather than mere pigmentation.

**25:26 — *Ya'aqov* (יַעֲקֹב) — "The Heel-Grasper":**
The name *ya'ăqōb* (יַעֲקֹב) is explained by the narrator via the action at birth: Jacob was born holding the *'āqēb* (עָקֵב, "heel") of Esau. The name is thus a participial form: *ya'ăqōb* = "he grasps the heel." The verb *'āqab* (עָקַב) means "to grasp by the heel" but also, in a derived sense, "to supplant, to circumvent, to follow closely at the heel." The double meaning — literal (heel-grasping at birth) and metaphorical (supplanting, deceiving) — is exploited throughout the Jacob narrative. Esau himself will pun on the name in Gen 27:36: *hăkî qārā' šĕmô ya'ăqōb wayyaqqĕbēnî zeh pa'amayim* — "Is it because his name is called Jacob that he has circumvented/supplanted me these two times?" The LXX Vaticanus renders *ya'ăqōb* as *Iakōb* (Ἰακώβ) throughout without translation, treating it as a proper name; the foot-grasping etymology is noted in the narrative (*epelambomenos tēs pternēs Esau*, "grasping the heel of Esau").

**25:27 — *Tam* (תָּם) — "Complete / Whole / Blameless":**
The description of Jacob as *'îš tām* (אִישׁ תָּם) — "a complete/whole/blameless man" — is one of the most theologically freighted adjectives in the book. The word *tām* (תָּם) from the root *tāmam* ("to be complete, whole, finished, without defect") is used of Noah in Gen 6:9 (*'îš ṣaddîq tāmîm hāyāh bĕdōrōtāyw*, "a righteous man, blameless in his generations") and of Job (Job 1:1: *'îš tām wĕyāšār*, "a complete and upright man"). The word carries moral-ethical force: wholeness of character, integrity, innocence. To call Jacob *tām* is startling given the narrative that follows — Jacob who deceives his father, flees from his brother, and is himself deceived by Laban. Whether the narrator uses *tām* with irony, or whether it is a straightforward characterization that the subsequent narrative complicates, is left open. The pairing with "dwelling in tents" (*yōšēb 'ohālîm*) contrasts Jacob with the field-dwelling, actively-hunting Esau and suggests the domestic, perhaps scholarly or contemplative, character of the tent-dweller. The LXX Vaticanus renders *tām* as *aplastos* (ἄπλαστος) — "unfeigned, guileless, without artifice" — an interpretive choice emphasizing sincerity and lack of craftiness, which sits in its own tension with the Jacob who follows.

**25:28 — "Because game was in his mouth" (*kî ṣayid bĕpîw*, כִּי-צַיִד בְּפִיו):**
The reason given for Isaac's love of Esau — "because game was in his mouth" — is grammatically ambiguous. *Bĕpîw* ("in his mouth") most naturally refers to Isaac's mouth: Isaac loved Esau because the taste of game was in his (Isaac's) own mouth — i.e., because he had a taste for the game Esau provided. Some commentators read *bĕpîw* as Esau's mouth — Isaac loved Esau because of what Esau said (smooth talk) — but this is the minority reading. The majority reading, preserved in most translations, understands *ṣayid bĕpîw* as "game [that went into] his mouth," making Isaac's love gastronomically conditioned. This contrasts sharply with Rebekah's love for Jacob — no reason is given for hers, which may imply it is unconditional. The parental division of love along lines of temperament and appetite is one of the narrative's quietly devastating observations about the Isaac household. EMET preserves the literal phrasing without smoothing the ambiguity.

**25:30 — *Ha'adom ha'adom* (הָאָדֹם הָאָדֹם) — The Double Red:**
Esau's demand in v. 30 is one of the most colloquially vivid lines in Genesis. He does not ask for "stew" or "lentil soup" — he demands *ha'adom ha'adom hazeh* — "the red — the red — the this one!" The doubling of *'ādom* ("red") conveys breathless, tunnel-vision craving: he cannot even name what he wants, only its color, twice. The word *hal'îṭēnî* (הַלְעִיטֵנִי, "let me swallow down, let me gulp") is also notable — it is the word used for force-feeding animals. Esau is not asking to eat; he is asking to be stuffed, like an animal at a feed-trough. The narrator's etymology follows immediately: "therefore his name was called Edom" — *'Ĕdôm* = *'ādom* = "red." The name Edom for the nation descending from Esau is thus anchored to this moment of impulsive appetite: Edom is born from Esau's inability to see beyond the color of a bowl of stew.

**25:34 — The Narrator's Verdict — "And Esau despised his birthright" (*wayyibez 'ēśāw 'et-habbĕkōrāh*):**
The final clause of the chapter is one of the sharpest authorial verdicts in Genesis. The verb *bāzāh* (בָּזָה, "to despise, to hold in contempt, to treat as worthless") is the same word used for contempt of God (Num 15:31), contempt of the word of the LORD (2 Sam 12:9), and contempt of parents (Prov 15:20). After the bare four-verb sequence — *wayyō'kal wayyēšt wayyāqom wayyēlak*, "he ate, he drank, he arose, he went" — the narrator adds the devastating *wayyibez*, "and he despised." There is no remorse, no hesitation, no narrative sympathy for Esau. The transaction is complete and Esau has moved on without a backward glance. The narrator's verdict hangs over the rest of the Jacob-Esau cycle: whatever Esau says or feels in subsequent chapters, the birthright was *already despised* — the moment of interior contempt was here, at this meal, before Esau even knew what it would cost him. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Esau de ētimasen ta prōtotokia* (Ησαυ δὲ ἠτίμασεν τὰ πρωτοτόκια) — "Esau dishonored/treated as worthless the birthright" — *atimazō* ("to dishonor, to treat as without value") is a precise equivalent of *bāzāh*; the full force of the contempt is present in both traditions.

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 21–22):**
Codex Vaticanus pages 21–22 carry Genesis 25 in continuous three-column uncial. Page 21 (online viewer 0025) opens mid-Genesis 24 with the chapter heading *CAP. XXXVIII* at the top center (ancient Greek chapter enumeration); the Genesis 25 text begins in the lower portion of the page and continues through approximately verse 20. Page 22 (online viewer 0026) carries the text from approximately verse 20 through Genesis 26, with the marginal notation *CAP. XXXIX* visible marking the ancient chapter division for Genesis 25 in the Greek enumeration. Key LXX variants and readings for this chapter:

1. **25:1** — LXX *prosthemenos de Abraam* (προσθέμενος δὲ Αβρααμ) — "and Abraham adding [he took a wife]" — precisely equivalent to MT *wayyōsep... wayyiqqaḥ*.

2. **25:6** — LXX *tais pallakaîs* (ταῖς παλλακαῖς) — "the concubines" — plural *pallakaí*, matching MT *happîlagšîm* in its plural form.

3. **25:8** — LXX *prosetethē pros to genos autou* (προσετέθη πρὸς τὸ γένος αὐτοῦ) — "he was added to his kindred" — for MT *ne'esaf el-'ammav*. The LXX uses *genos* ("kindred, family, race") where MT has *'am* ("people"). The verb *prostithēmi* ("to add, to attach") precisely captures the Niphal-of-'āsap sense of being "gathered in."

4. **25:18** — LXX *katenanti pantōn tōn adelphōn autou katōkēsen* (κατέναντι πάντων τῶν ἀδελφῶν αὐτοῦ κατῴκησεν) — "he dwelt over against all his brothers" — the LXX reads MT *nāpal* ("fell") as "dwelt/settled" (*katōkēsen*), harmonizing with Gen 16:12 (*yiškōn*, "he shall dwell"). This is a significant interpretive choice, softening the potentially ominous *nāpal* into a settlement formula.

5. **25:21** — LXX *eisēkousen autou ho theos* — "God heard him" — rendering the reciprocal *wayyē'āter lô* ("the LORD was entreated by him") as simple divine hearing, losing the responsive-relational nuance of the Niphal *'ātar*.

6. **25:22** — LXX *eskirtōn de ta paidia en autē* — "the children leaped within her" — using *skirtaō* (joyful leaping, the same verb in Luke 1:41 for John's prenatal movement) rather than any equivalent of the violent *rāṣaṣ* ("to crush"). A theologically significant softening of the MT's violent language.

7. **25:23** — LXX *ho meizōn douleusai tō elassoni* (ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι) — "the greater shall serve the lesser" — resolves the MT's syntactic ambiguity (*rav ya'avod tsa'ir*) unambiguously via case-marking: *tō elassoni* (dative) is clearly the one served, *ho meizōn* (nominative) is the subject. The LXX reading is the one Paul cites in Romans 9:12.

8. **25:25** — LXX *purrakes holos hōsei dora daseia* (πυρράκης ὅλος ὡσεὶ δορὰ δασεῖα) — "wholly ruddy/reddish like a hairy skin" — rendering *'admōnî kullô kĕ'aderet śē'ār*. The LXX uses *purrakes* ("reddish, rust-colored") for *'admōnî*, linking the description precisely to fire-red coloring.

9. **25:27** — LXX *aplastos* (ἄπλαστος) for MT *tām* — "guileless, unfeigned, without artifice" — as noted above, an interpretive choice emphasizing sincerity rather than the MT's fuller range of "complete/blameless."

10. **25:34** — LXX *ētimasen* (ἠτίμασεν) — "dishonored" — for MT *wayyibez* ("despised"). Both verbs convey contemptuous dismissal of the birthright's value.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, 4QGen-b may preserve fragments near Genesis 25, but no confirmed extant text for this specific chapter from the Dead Sea Scrolls has been identified in the current source materials. The Leningrad Codex (primary) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary) are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.
